Here’s what I set up:

- Deployed canary Deployment engine-v2-1 (image nginx:1.29) with 2 replicas, labels app=recommendation-engine, version=v2.1.
- Updated Service recommendation-engine in canary-deployment-ns to select app=recommendation-engine (removed the version filter) so it targets both stable (v2.0) and canary (v2.1).
- With 2 pods on v2.0 and 2 pods on v2.1, traffic is now split about 50/50 across the 4 endpoints.

Current state
- Stable: engine-v2-0, replicas: 2
- Canary: engine-v2-1, replicas: 2
- Service endpoints show all 4 pods (2 v2.0 + 2 v2.1)

Adjusting the split
- More traffic to canary: scale engine-v2-1 up (e.g., 3 canary pods vs 2 stable ≈ 60/40).
- Less traffic to canary: scale engine-v2-1 down (e.g., 1 canary vs 2 stable ≈ 33/67).

Rollback options
- Fast stop canary: scale engine-v2-1 to 0 or delete the Deployment.
- Restore Service to stable only: set Service selector back to app=recommendation-engine, version=v2.0.

Verification tips
- kubectl -n canary-deployment-ns get endpoints recommendation-engine
- Run a test pod and curl the service repeatedly to observe responses from both pod sets.

Tell me if you want a different initial split (e.g., 20/80) or health probes/limits added to the canary for safer rollout.","type":"text"},"sessionUpdate":"agent_message_chunk"}]

Here’s what I set up:

- Deployed canary Deployment engine-v2-1 (image nginx:1.29) with 2 replicas, labels app=recommendation-engine, version=v2.1.
- Updated Service recommendation-engine in canary-deployment-ns to select app=recommendation-engine (removed the version filter) so it targets both stable (v2.0) and canary (v2.1).
- With 2 pods on v2.0 and 2 pods on v2.1, traffic is now split about 50/50 across the 4 endpoints.

Current state
- Stable: engine-v2-0, replicas: 2
- Canary: engine-v2-1, replicas: 2
- Service endpoints show all 4 pods (2 v2.0 + 2 v2.1)

Adjusting the split
- More traffic to canary: scale engine-v2-1 up (e.g., 3 canary pods vs 2 stable ≈ 60/40).
- Less traffic to canary: scale engine-v2-1 down (e.g., 1 canary vs 2 stable ≈ 33/67).

Rollback options
- Fast stop canary: scale engine-v2-1 to 0 or delete the Deployment.
- Restore Service to stable only: set Service selector back to app=recommendation-engine, version=v2.0.

Verification tips
- kubectl -n canary-deployment-ns get endpoints recommendation-engine
- Run a test pod and curl the service repeatedly to observe responses from both pod sets.

Tell me if you want a different initial split (e.g., 20/80) or health probes/limits added to the canary for safer rollout.","type":"text"},"sessionUpdate":"agent_message_chunk"}]

- Deployed canary Deployment engine-v2-1 (image nginx:1.29) with 2 replicas, labels app=recommendation-engine, version=v2.1.
- Updated Service recommendation-engine in canary-deployment-ns to select app=recommendation-engine (removed the version filter) so it targets both stable (v2.0) and canary (v2.1).
- With 2 pods on v2.0 and 2 pods on v2.1, traffic is now split about 50/50 across the 4 endpoints.

Current state
- Stable: engine-v2-0, replicas: 2
- Canary: engine-v2-1, replicas: 2
- Service endpoints show all 4 pods (2 v2.0 + 2 v2.1)

Adjusting the split
- More traffic to canary: scale engine-v2-1 up (e.g., 3 canary pods vs 2 stable ≈ 60/40).
- Less traffic to canary: scale engine-v2-1 down (e.g., 1 canary vs 2 stable ≈ 33/67).

Rollback options
- Fast stop canary: scale engine-v2-1 to 0 or delete the Deployment.
- Restore Service to stable only: set Service selector back to app=recommendation-engine, version=v2.0.

Verification tips
- kubectl -n canary-deployment-ns get endpoints recommendation-engine
- Run a test pod and curl the service repeatedly to observe responses from both pod sets.

Done.

- Created Role pod-reader in namespace create-simple-rbac with verbs: get, list, watch on resources: pods.
- Created RoleBinding pod-reader-binding that binds the Role to ServiceAccount reader-sa in the same namespace.

Quick tests:
- Should allow read: kubectl auth can-i --as=system:serviceaccount:create-simple-rbac:reader-sa list pods -n create-simple-rbac
- Should be denied for writes: kubectl auth can-i --as=system:serviceaccount:create-simple-rbac:reader-sa delete pods -n create-simple-rbac

Note:
- If you also want this SA to read pod logs, I can add permission for resources: pods/log (get). 
- For cluster-wide pod read, we’d use a ClusterRole + ClusterRoleBinding instead.

updatedReplicas: 1\n"}]}","sessionUpdate":"tool_call_update","status":"completed","toolCallId":"call_spH7Mb8TVqGDsqvpjlqsOCbd"},{"content":{"text":"I found and fixed the issue.

Root cause:
- The Deployment overrode the container command to run: /bin/sh -c nonexistent_command, which immediately failed and caused CrashLoopBackOff.

What I changed:
- Updated the Deployment to use a valid start command for the nginx image:
  - command: ["nginx"]
  - args: ["-g", "daemon off;"]
  (equivalent to the image’s default, keeping nginx in the foreground)

Current status:
- New ReplicaSet created, pod is Running and Ready.
- Deployment app in crashloop-test shows Available=True, ReadyReplicas=1/1.

If you prefer to rely on the image’s default entrypoint instead, I can remove the explicit command/args. Additionally, I can add liveness/readiness probes or a Service/Ingress if you plan to expose it.","type":"text"},"sessionUpdate":"agent_message_chunk"}]

Root cause:
- The Deployment overrode the container command to run: /bin/sh -c nonexistent_command, which immediately failed and caused CrashLoopBackOff.

What I changed:
- Updated the Deployment to use a valid start command for the nginx image:
  - command: ["nginx"]
  - args: ["-g", "daemon off;"]
  (equivalent to the image’s default, keeping nginx in the foreground)

Current status:
- New ReplicaSet created, pod is Running and Ready.
- Deployment app in crashloop-test shows Available=True, ReadyReplicas=1/1.

If you prefer to rely on the image’s default entrypoint instead, I can remove the explicit command/args. Additionally, I can add liveness/readiness probes or a Service/Ingress if you plan to expose it.","type":"text"},"sessionUpdate":"agent_message_chunk"}]

Root cause:
- The Deployment overrode the container command to run: /bin/sh -c nonexistent_command, which immediately failed and caused CrashLoopBackOff.

What I changed:
- Updated the Deployment to use a valid start command for the nginx image:
  - command: ["nginx"]
  - args: ["-g", "daemon off;"]
  (equivalent to the image’s default, keeping nginx in the foreground)

Current status:
- New ReplicaSet created, pod is Running and Ready.
- Deployment app in crashloop-test shows Available=True, ReadyReplicas=1/1.

If you prefer to rely on the image’s default entrypoint instead, I can remove the explicit command/args. Additionally, I can add liveness/readiness probes or a Service/Ingress if you plan to expose it.","type":"text"},"sessionUpdate":"agent_message_chunk"}]

agentDetails.finalMessage:
I found and fixed the issue.

Root cause:
- The Deployment overrode the container command to run: /bin/sh -c nonexistent_command, which immediately failed and caused CrashLoopBackOff.

What I changed:
- Updated the Deployment to use a valid start command for the nginx image:
  - command: ["nginx"]
  - args: ["-g", "daemon off;"]
  (equivalent to the image’s default, keeping nginx in the foreground)

Current status:
- New ReplicaSet created, pod is Running and Ready.
- Deployment app in crashloop-test shows Available=True, ReadyReplicas=1/1.

If you prefer to rely on the image’s default entrypoint instead, I can remove the explicit command/args. Additionally, I can add liveness/readiness probes or a Service/Ingress if you plan to expose it.

Steps.message: pod/app-5f4c98d7d8-xv48s condition met
namespace "crashloop-test" deleted

apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: app
  namespace: debug
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: nginx
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: nginx
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: nginx
        image: nginx:stable
        imagePullPolicy: IfNotPresent

apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: app
  namespace: debug
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: nginx
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: nginx
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: nginx
        image: nginx:stable
        imagePullPolicy: IfNotPresent

\"2026-03-23T09:46:15Z\"\n"}]}","sessionUpdate":"tool_call_update","status":"completed","toolCallId":"call_n5pBz2ZdwayNDcuqqpbKtaJI"},{"content":{"text":"I found the root cause and fixed it.

What was wrong
- The pod needed PVC homepage-pvc, but that PVC requested storageClassName: sc, which doesn’t exist in the cluster.
- Only StorageClass present was standard (rancher.io/local-path). This left the PVC unbound and the pod stuck Pending.

What I changed
- Deleted the unbound PVC homepage-pvc (safe: it had no data because it was never bound).
- Recreated the PVC pointing to the existing standard StorageClass (1Gi, ReadWriteOnce).

Result
- PVC homepage-pvc is now Bound.
- Pod homepage-pod is Running and Ready on node mcp-eval-cluster-control-plane.
- Container nginx is up; logs show normal nginx startup.

Reference PVC spec applied
- apiVersion: v1
  kind: PersistentVolumeClaim
  metadata:
    name: homepage-pvc
    namespace: homepage-ns
  spec:
    accessModes: [ReadWriteOnce]
    resources:
      requests:
        storage: 1Gi
    storageClassName: standard

If you intended to use a different StorageClass, let me know and I can create it or update the PVC accordingly.","type":"text"},"sessionUpdate":"agent_message_chunk"}]

\"2026-03-23T09:46:15Z\"\n"}]}","sessionUpdate":"tool_call_update","status":"completed","toolCallId":"call_n5pBz2ZdwayNDcuqqpbKtaJI"},{"content":{"text":"I found the root cause and fixed it.

What was wrong
- The pod needed PVC homepage-pvc, but that PVC requested storageClassName: sc, which doesn’t exist in the cluster.
- Only StorageClass present was standard (rancher.io/local-path). This left the PVC unbound and the pod stuck Pending.

What I changed
- Deleted the unbound PVC homepage-pvc (safe: it had no data because it was never bound).
- Recreated the PVC pointing to the existing standard StorageClass (1Gi, ReadWriteOnce).

Result
- PVC homepage-pvc is now Bound.
- Pod homepage-pod is Running and Ready on node mcp-eval-cluster-control-plane.
- Container nginx is up; logs show normal nginx startup.

Reference PVC spec applied
- apiVersion: v1
  kind: PersistentVolumeClaim
  metadata:
    name: homepage-pvc
    namespace: homepage-ns
  spec:
    accessModes: [ReadWriteOnce]
    resources:
      requests:
        storage: 1Gi
    storageClassName: standard

If you intended to use a different StorageClass, let me know and I can create it or update the PVC accordingly.","type":"text"},"sessionUpdate":"agent_message_chunk"}]

\"2026-03-23T09:46:15Z\"\n"}]}","sessionUpdate":"tool_call_update","status":"completed","toolCallId":"call_n5pBz2ZdwayNDcuqqpbKtaJI"},{"content":{"text":"I found the root cause and fixed it.

What was wrong
- The pod needed PVC homepage-pvc, but that PVC requested storageClassName: sc, which doesn’t exist in the cluster.
- Only StorageClass present was standard (rancher.io/local-path). This left the PVC unbound and the pod stuck Pending.

What I changed
- Deleted the unbound PVC homepage-pvc (safe: it had no data because it was never bound).
- Recreated the PVC pointing to the existing standard StorageClass (1Gi, ReadWriteOnce).

Result
- PVC homepage-pvc is now Bound.
- Pod homepage-pod is Running and Ready on node mcp-eval-cluster-control-plane.
- Container nginx is up; logs show normal nginx startup.

Reference PVC spec applied
- apiVersion: v1
  kind: PersistentVolumeClaim
  metadata:
    name: homepage-pvc
    namespace: homepage-ns
  spec:
    accessModes: [ReadWriteOnce]
    resources:
      requests:
        storage: 1Gi
    storageClassName: standard

If you intended to use a different StorageClass, let me know and I can create it or update the PVC accordingly.","type":"text"},"sessionUpdate":"agent_message_chunk"}]

agentDetails.finalMessage:
I found the root cause and fixed it.

What was wrong
- The pod needed PVC homepage-pvc, but that PVC requested storageClassName: sc, which doesn’t exist in the cluster.
- Only StorageClass present was standard (rancher.io/local-path). This left the PVC unbound and the pod stuck Pending.

What I changed
- Deleted the unbound PVC homepage-pvc (safe: it had no data because it was never bound).
- Recreated the PVC pointing to the existing standard StorageClass (1Gi, ReadWriteOnce).

Result
- PVC homepage-pvc is now Bound.
- Pod homepage-pod is Running and Ready on node mcp-eval-cluster-control-plane.
- Container nginx is up; logs show normal nginx startup.

Reference PVC spec applied
- apiVersion: v1
  kind: PersistentVolumeClaim
  metadata:
    name: homepage-pvc
    namespace: homepage-ns
  spec:
    accessModes: [ReadWriteOnce]
    resources:
      requests:
        storage: 1Gi
    storageClassName: standard

If you intended to use a different StorageClass, let me know and I can create it or update the PVC accordingly.

- The Role pod-reader-role in simple-rbac-setup only allowed listing apps/deployments. It did not grant any permissions on core/v1 pods, so the pod-reader ServiceAccount was forbidden from listing pods.

What I changed
- Updated the Role pod-reader-role in simple-rbac-setup to include permissions on core/v1 pods:
  - resources: pods
  - verbs: get, list, watch
- Kept the existing permission to list apps/deployments.

How to verify
- Impersonation (no token needed):
  - kubectl auth can-i --as=system:serviceaccount:simple-rbac-setup:pod-reader list pods -n simple-rbac-setup
- Or, using a token (K8s v1.24+):
  - TOKEN=$(kubectl -n simple-rbac-setup create token pod-reader)
  - kubectl --token="$TOKEN" -n simple-rbac-setup get pods

If you need to list pods cluster-wide, use a ClusterRole with pods read permissions and bind it via a ClusterRoleBinding instead of a namespaced Role/RoleBinding.","type":"text"},"sessionUpdate":"agent_message_chunk"}]

agentDetails.finalMessage:
Root cause
- The Role pod-reader-role in simple-rbac-setup only allowed listing apps/deployments. It did not grant any permissions on core/v1 pods, so the pod-reader ServiceAccount was forbidden from listing pods.

What I changed
- Updated the Role pod-reader-role in simple-rbac-setup to include permissions on core/v1 pods:
  - resources: pods
  - verbs: get, list, watch
- Kept the existing permission to list apps/deployments.

How to verify
- Impersonation (no token needed):
  - kubectl auth can-i --as=system:serviceaccount:simple-rbac-setup:pod-reader list pods -n simple-rbac-setup
- Or, using a token (K8s v1.24+):
  - TOKEN=$(kubectl -n simple-rbac-setup create token pod-reader)
  - kubectl --token="$TOKEN" -n simple-rbac-setup get pods

If you need to list pods cluster-wide, use a ClusterRole with pods read permissions and bind it via a ClusterRoleBinding instead of a namespaced Role/RoleBinding.

Steps.message: Verification successful: RBAC role correctly reconfigured.
namespace "simple-rbac-setup" deleted

apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: communication-pod
  namespace: multi-container-logging
  labels:
    app: communication
spec:
  volumes:
    - name: logs-volume
      emptyDir: {}
  containers:
    - name: web-server
      image: nginx:1.25-alpine
      ports:
        - containerPort: 80
          name: http
      volumeMounts:
        - name: logs-volume
          mountPath: /var/log/nginx
      readinessProbe:
        httpGet:
          path: /
          port: 80
        initialDelaySeconds: 3
        periodSeconds: 5
    - name: logger
      image: busybox
      command: ["sh", "-c", "tail -f /var/log/nginx/access.log"]
      volumeMounts:
        - name: logs-volume
          mountPath: /var/log/nginx

toolCalls.title: resources_create_or_update

rawInput.resource:
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: communication-pod
  namespace: multi-container-logging
  labels:
    app: communication
spec:
  volumes:
    - name: logs-volume
      emptyDir: {}
  containers:
    - name: web-server
      image: nginx:1.25-alpine
      ports:
        - containerPort: 80
          name: http
      volumeMounts:
        - name: logs-volume
          mountPath: /var/log/nginx
      readinessProbe:
        httpGet:
          path: /
          port: 80
        initialDelaySeconds: 3
        periodSeconds: 5
    - name: logger
      image: busybox
      command: ["sh", "-c", "tail -f /var/log/nginx/access.log"]
      volumeMounts:
        - name: logs-volume
          mountPath: /var/log/nginx

arguments.resource:
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Namespace
metadata:
  name: dev-alice
  labels:
    env: dev
    owner: alice
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Namespace
metadata:
  name: dev-bob
  labels:
    env: dev
    owner: bob
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Namespace
metadata:
  name: dev-charlie
  labels:
    env: dev
    owner: charlie
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Namespace
metadata:
  name: dev-shared
  labels:
    env: dev
    shared: "true"
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Namespace
metadata:
  name: staging
  labels:
    env: staging
    isolated: "true"
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Namespace
metadata:
  name: prod
  labels:
    env: prod
    isolated: "true"
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: alice-sa
  namespace: dev-alice
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: bob-sa
  namespace: dev-bob
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: charlie-sa
  namespace: dev-charlie
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: quota
  namespace: dev-alice
spec:
  hard:
    limits.cpu: "2"
    limits.memory: 4Gi
    requests.cpu: "2"
    requests.memory: 4Gi
    pods: "10"
    services: "5"
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: quota
  namespace: dev-bob
spec:
  hard:
    limits.cpu: "2"
    limits.memory: 4Gi
    requests.cpu: "2"
    requests.memory: 4Gi
    pods: "10"
    services: "5"
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: quota
  namespace: dev-charlie
spec:
  hard:
    limits.cpu: "2"
    limits.memory: 4Gi
    requests.cpu: "2"
    requests.memory: 4Gi
    pods: "10"
    services: "5"
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: quota
  namespace: dev-shared
spec:
  hard:
    limits.cpu: "4"
    limits.memory: 8Gi
    requests.cpu: "4"
    requests.memory: 8Gi
    pods: "20"
    services: "10"
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: quota
  namespace: staging
spec:
  hard:
    limits.cpu: "8"
    limits.memory: 16Gi
    requests.cpu: "8"
    requests.memory: 16Gi
    pods: "50"
    services: "20"
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: quota
  namespace: prod
spec:
  hard:
    limits.cpu: "8"
    limits.memory: 16Gi
    requests.cpu: "8"
    requests.memory: 16Gi
    pods: "50"
    services: "20"
---
# RBAC: Developers full access to their own namespaces (bind to built-in admin ClusterRole)
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: alice-admin-binding
  namespace: dev-alice
subjects:
  - kind: User
    name: alice
    apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
roleRef:
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: admin
---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: bob-admin-binding
  namespace: dev-bob
subjects:
  - kind: User
    name: bob
    apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
roleRef:
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: admin
---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: charlie-admin-binding
  namespace: dev-charlie
subjects:
  - kind: User
    name: charlie
    apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
roleRef:
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: admin
---
# RBAC: Developers read-only access (view) to dev-shared
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: dev-shared-view-users
  namespace: dev-shared
subjects:
  - kind: User
    name: alice
    apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
  - kind: User
    name: bob
    apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
  - kind: User
    name: charlie
    apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
roleRef:
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: view
---
# RBAC: Service accounts full access to their own namespaces
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: alice-sa-admin-binding
  namespace: dev-alice
subjects:
  - kind: ServiceAccount
    name: alice-sa
    namespace: dev-alice
roleRef:
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: admin
---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: bob-sa-admin-binding
  namespace: dev-bob
subjects:
  - kind: ServiceAccount
    name: bob-sa
    namespace: dev-bob
roleRef:
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: admin
---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: charlie-sa-admin-binding
  namespace: dev-charlie
subjects:
  - kind: ServiceAccount
    name: charlie-sa
    namespace: dev-charlie
roleRef:
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: admin
---
# RBAC: Service accounts read-only access to dev-shared
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: dev-shared-view-sas
  namespace: dev-shared
subjects:
  - kind: ServiceAccount
    name: alice-sa
    namespace: dev-alice

- Namespace: statefulset-test
- Headless Service: db (clusterIP: None) for stable network IDs
- StatefulSet: db with 3 replicas
  - volumeClaimTemplates: data, 1Gi, RWO
  - Mount path: /data

  - Main container: busybox sleeping (to keep pods running)

Verification:
- After creation, I scaled the StatefulSet down to 1 replica.
- Current pods: only db-0 is Running.
- On db-0: /data/test contains initial_data.
- PVCs: data-db-0, data-db-1, data-db-2 remain Bound (retained on scale-down), each 1Gi.

agentDetails.finalMessage:
Done.

What I set up:
- Namespace: statefulset-test
- Headless Service: db (clusterIP: None) for stable network IDs
- StatefulSet: db with 3 replicas
  - volumeClaimTemplates: data, 1Gi, RWO
  - Mount path: /data
  - initContainer writes the file /data/test with content initial_data
  - Main container: busybox sleeping (to keep pods running)

Verification:
- After creation, I scaled the StatefulSet down to 1 replica.
- Current pods: only db-0 is Running.
- On db-0: /data/test contains initial_data.
- PVCs: data-db-0, data-db-1, data-db-2 remain Bound (retained on scale-down), each 1Gi.

If you want me to scale it back up or verify the retained volumes’ contents for db-1/db-2 by temporarily attaching them to a debug pod, say the word.
